title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered in wildlife rescue and monitoring. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Beth, The Hedgehog Carer
Beth fostered small injured hedgehogs in a heated shed behind her house after completing a rescue-centre course. [47]She had to weigh each animal at the same time every evening carefully because sudden changes could mean it was not eating enough.[/47] At first she became attached to the smallest ones and found release days upsetting, even when release meant success. Beth worked as a pharmacist, and [48]her careful habit of reading dosage instructions helped her avoid mistakes with tiny amounts of medicine.[/48] The smell and mess were worse than she had expected, especially during winter storms when extra temporary cages filled the shed and blocked the doorway. Her favourite patient was a hedgehog that refused food until she hid mealworms inside rolled newspaper tubes. Beth says wildlife care taught her that kindness is often repetitive: cleaning bowls, checking bedding and leaving animals alone when human attention would only frighten them further.
texts.B:
### Connor, The Seal Watcher
Connor eagerly joined a coastal volunteer seal-watching protection rota during pupping season on a narrow public beach. [49]He learned to stand farther from the beach than seemed strictly necessary, because nervous mothers might abandon pups if people gathered too close.[/49] Excited visitors sometimes accused him of spoiling photographs, but he carried binoculars so they could still see details safely without crossing the rope line. Connor had been a security guard, and his calm posture helped him hold a boundary without sounding aggressive or enjoying authority. [50]The most difficult conversation was with a tourist who wanted to rescue a pup that was simply waiting for its mother to return after feeding offshore.[/50] Connor radioed the official rescue centre for advice, then explained the situation very slowly. He says the role changed his idea of helping: sometimes the best immediate action is keeping distance, not rushing forward with good intentions too quickly.
texts.C:
### Lila, The Swift Nest Monitor
Lila carefully monitored swift nesting boxes fixed high under the eaves of old houses near the town centre. [51]She recorded activity from the opposite pavement because climbing ladders would have disturbed the birds and worried residents.[/51] The quiet work demanded attention to tiny clues, such as a feather caught near an nest entrance hole or repeated shadow movement. As an architect, Lila understood which renovations might accidentally block nest sites, so she began advising homeowners before building work started in spring. [52]Her biggest disappointment was discovering that one newly painted house had sealed a box days before the birds returned.[/52] She used that example in local leaflets, without naming the owners, who had acted unknowingly. Lila says the birds made her look upward in streets she thought she knew completely from daily walks. [53]She now celebrates success only when chicks are seen leaving the nest, not merely when adults arrive.[/53]
texts.D:
### Raj, The Night Driver
Raj transported injured wildlife from callers' homes to a volunteer vet late at night when ordinary services were closed. [54]He kept cardboard boxes, towels and gloves in the boot because callers often had nothing suitable for carrying an animal safely.[/54] His taxi-driving experience helped him find rural addresses with poor signs, but farm tracks still tested his patience in bad weather. [55]The hardest rescue involved a swan on a dark lane, where he had to wait for police to stop traffic before approaching it.[/55] Raj learned not to promise good outcomes, only that the animal would be assessed quickly by someone qualified. He sometimes felt invisible because he handed patients over and left before treatment began behind the surgery door. [56]He now asks the vet for brief updates so callers can be told what happened after the journey.[/56] He says that information matters to people who felt helpless at the roadside after calling for help.

questions.question: followed a fixed weighing routine to spot possible feeding problems?
questions.explanation: Beth weighed each hedgehog at the same time each evening to notice sudden changes.

questions.question: used professional care with medication instructions to avoid dosage errors?
questions.explanation: Beth's pharmacy habits helped her avoid mistakes with tiny amounts of medicine.

questions.question: kept people back because getting too close could make parents leave their young?
questions.explanation: Connor stood far from the beach so seal mothers would not abandon pups.

questions.question: had to explain that an apparently abandoned young animal should not be taken away?
questions.explanation: Connor dealt with a tourist who wanted to rescue a pup waiting for its mother.

questions.question: observed from ground level to avoid disturbing animals and residents?
questions.explanation: Lila recorded activity from the pavement rather than using ladders.

questions.question: was upset that building maintenance had blocked a nesting place before migration?
questions.explanation: Lila found a painted house had sealed a box before birds returned.

questions.question: regards success as confirmed only when young birds are seen leaving?
questions.explanation: Lila celebrates when chicks leave the nest, not just when adults arrive.

questions.question: carried basic containers and protective items because callers were often unprepared?
questions.explanation: Raj kept boxes, towels and gloves because callers lacked safe carrying equipment.

questions.question: needed official traffic control before approaching an injured animal?
questions.explanation: Raj waited for police to stop traffic before approaching a swan.

questions.question: requests follow-up information so people can learn the result of a rescue?
questions.explanation: Raj asks the vet for updates so callers can be told what happened.
